VLANs

VLANs are primarily used to provide network segmentation at layer 2. VLANs enable the grouping of users
by logical function instead of physical location. They make managing bandwidth usage within networks
possible by:

•  Allowing grouping of high-bandwidth users on low-traffic segments

•  Organizing users from different LAN segments according to their need for common resources and

individual protocols

Public

VLANs 319

•

Improving traffic control at the edge of networks by separating traffic of different protocol types.

•  Enhancing network security by creating subnets to control in-band access to specific network resources

VLANs are generally assigned on an organizational basis rather than on a physical basis. For example, a
network administrator could assign all workstations and servers used by a particular workgroup to the same
VLAN, regardless of their physical locations.

Hosts in the same VLAN can directly communicate with one another. A router or a Layer 3 switch is required
for hosts in different VLANs to communicate with one another.

VLANs help reduce bandwidth waste, improve LAN security, and enable network administrators to address
issues such as scalability and network management.

NOTE

Refer to the Layer 2 Bridging Guide for VLAN configuration and commands.

Precision Time Protocol (PTP)

NOTE

PTP is only supported on the HPE Aruba Networking 8325P, 8360, and 9300
Switch Series (R8Z96A model only).

NOTE

If PTP is configured on MACsec-supported ports, the switch can experience
higher jitter or lower PTP accuracy.

Precision Time Protocol (PTP) is defined in the IEEE 1588 standard (Standard for a Precision Clock
Synchronization Protocol for Networked Measurement and Control Systems). PTP synchronizes clocks in
packet-based networks that include distributed device clocks of varying precision and stability. On a local
area network, it achieves clock accuracy in the sub-microsecond range, making it suitable for measurement
and control systems. PTP is currently employed to synchronize financial transactions, mobile phone tower
transmissions, and networks that require precise timing but lack access to satellite navigation signals.

PTP is not supported on EVPN/VXLAN tunnels.

PTP clocks

A PTP network consists of PTP-enabled devices and devices that are not using PTP. The PTP-enabled
devices typically consist of clock-aware devices such as ordinary clocks (which are usually single-port
end-stations) and one or more grandsource clocks, transparent clocks, and boundary clocks (multi‑port
L2/L3 time‑aware devices).

The basic clock node types include:

Public

Precision Time Protocol (PTP) 320

Grandsource clock - A grandsource clock is the primary source of time for the downstream devices. This is a
device with greater clock quality which may have direct access to a reference clock.

Ordinary clock - An ordinary clock is a single port end-station (which can include a GM as the originating
PTP time-aware device).

Transparent clock (8360 Switch Series only) - A transparent clock can have multiple network port
connections but it does not act as either a clock-source or a clock-sink. Rather, it updates the correction
field within the PTP event messages (SYNC/FOLLOW_UP, DELAY_REQUEST) to compensate for the transit
time delay. Transparent clocks compensate for switch latency and jitter, making network devices appear
transparent to other PTP time-aware devices. They help in reducing the end-station time errors and
improving synchronization quality. But a transparent clock itself does not synchronize its time.

There are two types of transparent clocks:

•  End-to-End (E2E) transparent clock—Updates the correctionField field in the PTP messages with the
total time the PTP packet was resident in the network device. This is called resident time correction.

•  Peer-to-Peer (P2P) transparent clock—Updates the correctionField field in the PTP messages with
the sum of upstream link delay and the resident time. The upstream link delay is the estimated packet
propagation delay between the upstream neighbor P2P clock and the local transparent clock.

Boundary clock—The boundary clock implements a local PTP clock where one port will act as clock-sink
which synchronizes itself with the clock-source while other ports act as clock-source ports to its downstream
clock-aware devices. The clock-source port is used to redistribute the clock to another set of clock-sinks. The
best master clock algorithm (BMCA) is used by the boundary clock to select the best configured acceptable
clock-source clock.

Subtopics

Best master clock algorithm
Configuration examples
Configuration recommendations
Use cases
PTP commands
SyncE commands

Best master clock algorithm

About this task

The BMCA behavior changes per profile, but is usually similar to the specified in IEEE 1588-2008 section
9.3.

The clocks broadcast information about their properties (the datasets), which is then used as an input to the
selection process. These are the values used in the BMCA, sorted by descending order of preference, as in
IEEE 1588-2008:

Public

Best master clock algorithm 321

Procedure

1.  grandmasterIdentity: If two datasets suggest the same GM, the one with the least hops, the lower
clockIdentity or the lower portIdentity will be chosen. This shall return an error if using a loopback
connection.

2.  priority1: An absolute priority that can be configured by the user (this value is fixed to 128 in G.8275.1

and G.8275.2).

3.  clockClass: Attribute defining a clock's TAI traceability, synchronization state and expected

performance.

4.  clockAccuracy: Attribute defining the local accuracy of a clock.

5.  offsetScaledLogVariance: An attribute defining the stability of a clock.

6.  priority2: User-configurable priority that can be used as a tiebreaker when the other attributes match.

7.  clockIdentity: A final tiebreaker consisting of the MAC address of the clock.

Results

In all the inputs, the lower number is preferred, e.g. a clock with a priorty1 value of 90 is better than 100.
The algorithm runs periodically, so that the device reacts quickly to any attribute changes.

The IEEE 1588 standard allows for an alternate BMCA, which can be used in cases where the default
algorithm presents problems or would not suffice the requirements of the network.

The ITU-T G.8275.1-2022 section 6.3.7 and G.8275.2-2022 section 6.7.9 specify their own alternate
BMCA, considering a localPriority value, which is currently non-configurable, therefore it is set to the
recommended default of 128. The way the grandmasterIdentity and clock class are used as tiebreakers
differs against IEEE 1588 too.

NOTE

The BCMA is now called the Best timeTransmitter Clock Algorithm (BTCA) on
newer revisions of the ITU-T standards.

PTP network diagram

The following diagram illustrates how the various PTP clock nodes are connected and how the timing
information flows from the origin to the end‐stations to achieve the time synchronization. This diagram
depicts the PTP clocks in a source‐sink hierarchy.

Figure 1. Example PTP network with clocks in a source‐sink hierarchy

Public

Best master clock algorithm 322

Configuration examples

Configuring an end-to-end transparent clock (TC E2E)

Follow these steps to configure the E2E transparent clock:

1.  Configure the mandatory commands:

Public

Configuration examples 323

a.  Configure the PTP mode and the delay mechanism.

b.  Configure the PTP clock-step mode.

c.  Configure the transport protocol.

d.  Configure PTP globally.

Switch config:

switch(config)# ptp profile aes67

switch(config-ptp)# mode transparent end-to-end

switch(config-ptp)# clock-step one-step

switch(config-ptp)# transport-protocol ip

switch(config-ptp)# enable

2.  Enable PTP on the connected interfaces :
switch(config)# int 1/1/1

switch(config-if)# ptp enable

switch(config-if)# int 1/1/2

switch(config-if)# ptp enable

Configuring an end-to-end boundary clock (BC E2E)

Follow these steps to configure the end-to-end boundary clock:

1.  Create the PTP context using the specified profile.
switch(config)# ptp profile aes67

2.  Configure the mandatory commands:

a.  Configure the PTP mode.

b.  Configure the PTP clock-step mode.

c.  Configure the transport protocol.

d.  Configure PTP globally.

switch(config-ptp)# mode boundary end-to-end

switch(config-ptp)# clock-step one-step

switch(config-ptp)# transport-protocol ip

switch(config-ptp)# enable

3.  Configure the optional commands which participate in the best clock source algorithm:

a.  Configure priority1 value.

Public

Configuration examples 324

b.  Configure priority2 value.

switch(config-ptp)# priority1 1

switch(config-ptp)# priority2 10

4.  Enable PTP on the connected interfaces:
switch(config)# int 1/1/1

switch(config-if)# ptp enable

switch(config-if)# int 1/1/2

switch(config-if)# ptp enable

5.  Optional: changing packet interval rate for various PTP parameters:
switch(config-if)# ptp sync-interval 1588v2 1

switch(config-if)# ptp announce-interval 1588v2 -5

switch(config-if)# ptp announce-timeout 1588v2 4

switch(config-if)# ptp delay-req-interval 1588v2 -3

PTPv1 passthrough

NOTE

Only supported on the 8360 Switch Series.

As part of the PTPv1 passthrough feature, PTPv1 packets are allowed to pass through both PTP and
non-PTP aware devices. PTPv1 traffic is forwarded based on the destination MAC address and the switches
do not process or route the PTPv1 traffic.

NOTE

As per the IEEE standard, PTPv1 packets are expected to have a TTL value of 0,
which makes them operational only in LAN environment and non-routable.

PTP support over VSX

NOTE
Only supported on the 8360 Switch Series.

PTP Transparent Clock is supported on VSX and supports both P2P and E2E delay mechanisms. The
PTP-TC clock configuration is synchronized between VSX peers.

•  PTP must be enabled and configured on the Inter Switch Link (ISL) so that the PTP packets bridged

through the ISL have the correction field (CF) updated. In case of peer-to-peer (P2P) delay mechanism,
the link-delay for the ISL is also determined by the PTP protocol and compensated in the CF.

Public

Configuration examples 325

•  PTP for a keepalive (KA) link depends on L3 configuration. If the routing protocol is configured to

forward PTP packets through a KA link, then PTP must be enabled on the KA link. In general, it is not
recommended to send L3 routed packets through a keepalive link.

Missing SyncE clock source

NOTE

SyncE is only supported on the 8325P Switch Series.

When a device loses connection with a SyncE clock source there will be a transitional period while the
algorithm selects a new clock source from the available options. During this period (typically not more than
a few seconds) the QL of the device and devices synchronized downstream may change to an unexpected
value. If new clock source is selected, then the final state will show the QL of the new selected source. If
there are no other suitable sources the device will move to holdover state and track the internal clock or local
oscillator.

Limitations, conflicts, and exclusions

On 8360 Switch Series:

•  Peer-to-peer transparent clock requires the clock-domain configuration for the peer-delay packets
exchange, whereas the end-to-end transparent clock operates across domains. So the clock-domain
configuration is not considered for end-to-end transparent clock.

•  With peer-to-peer delay-mechanism (especially two-step), having more than one end client on the same

link is not a valid configuration. It is recommended to have only 1:1 peer-to-peer communication.

•

If peer-delay packets are made to transit any intermediate nodes (eg. through unicast config or
intermediate L2 node, etc.) then it can have higher noise due to non-compensated resident time.

•  With IPv4, ECMP is not supported on PTP BC.

•  When PTP is disabled on a port, the switch behaves as PTP unaware device. The forwarding rules

applicable on the port will take effect based on the transport protocol and the port configuration for the
incoming PTP messages.

•  When software downgrade is performed from 10.15 and above to versions lower than 10.15, ptp vlan
configuration will not be retained. Users need to configure ptp vlan manually again after downgrade.

On the 8325P and 9300 Switch Series:

•  Unicast and multicast configurations conflict. In the case where both are configured on the system,

unicast will be used and multicast will be ignored.

•  To use multicast transmission, all unicast configuration must be removed. If Unicast and multicast are
configured together, all Multicast ports will have their states set to Disabled, with the PTP fault reason
"Unicast - Multicast configuration conflict". An WARN event log will also be displayed:

Public

Configuration examples 326

PTP Unicast and Multicast are both configured. Only Unicast will be

honored. If Multicast operation is desired, remove all PTP Unicast

configuration.

•  When conflicting configurations are entered via CLI, the user will be warned via a printed message. In
the case of a unicast port being configured when there are multicast ports configured, the following
message will be displayed:

Warning: PTP Unicast is configured on this interface, but there are still

PTP Multicast ports configured. PTP Multicast ports will not work until all

'ptp peer ip' configurations are removed.

•

In the case of a multicast port being configured when there are Unicast ports configured, the following
message will be displayed:

Warning: PTP is not operational on this interface.Configure 'ptp peer ip'

or remove all other 'ptp peer ip' configuration to make it operational.

•  Transparent mode and two-step clock can be configured via REST but are not supported.

The following are not supported on the 8325P, 8360, and 9300 Switch Series:

•

IPv6 support

•  SMPTE mixed mode

The following are not supported on the 8325P and 9300 Switch Series:

•  Peer-to-peer mode

•  Transparent clock mode

•  Two step option for Clock step.

•  VSX/VSF is not supported along with PTP on this platform.

•  Only default VRF is supported.

•  Multicast and Unicast ports in the same configuration.

On the 8325P and 9300 Switch Series:

•  PTP configurations require restarting the PTP service to apply, which can affect the PTP operation at

system level and the PTP setting at port level.The following PTP configurations are the only exceptions:

◦  Priority1 and Priority2 setting for profile

◦  PTP VLAN setting at port level.

On the 8325P Switch Series:

Public

Configuration examples 327

•  SyncE configurations require restarting the SyncE service to apply, which can affect the SyncE operation

to reconverage based on the wait-to-restore timer configured. The downstream device can also be
affected until the system is fully converged.

•  LAG membership reconfiguration with SyncE configuration also requires the SyncE service to restart. If a

port without any SyncE configuration is assigned to a LAG, it doesn't affect the SyncE service.

•  Disable RS-FEC on the PTP enabled 25G port to maintain Class C performance. The 25GbE port with

RS-FEC enabled may cause a higher PTP timestamp error. Additionally, enabling and disabling adjacent
ports (with RS-FEC) increases the timestamp error and may generate a latency change.

•  RS-FEC must be disabled on peer device also to establish link. Configuring the RS-FEC setting will

bounce the port.

•  Mirroring on egress is not supported for any packets transmitted for CPU. Do not use mirror to capture

PTP protocol packets on egress. Mirror PTP protocol packets on ingress is supported.

Hardware considerations

PTP is supported on breakout cables. PTP connections through a QSA28 (QSFP28 to SFP adapter)
transceivers are not supported. DACs are not supported for use through QSA28 adapters. (Refer to the
Transceiver Guide for further information.)

PTP Clients connected to 1 Gbps XCVRs on an 8360 Switch acting as the PTP boundary clock or PTP
transparent clock will have a slightly higher offset when there is background data traffic between clients.
This is a known limitation specifically to Clients connected only on 1 Gbps links on the HPE Aruba
Networking8360 Switch Series. Clients connected on higher speed links (10/25/40/50/100) Gbps will not
be impacted.

NOTE

If PTP is configured on MACsec-supported ports, the switch can experience
higher jitter or lower PTP accuracy.

Configuration recommendations

PTP CoPP class configuration recommendations

Configuration recommendations for a boundary clock

The PTP CoPP class must be adjusted based on the number of clients associated with the boundary clock
and the configured packet rate. For example, if there are 1000 clients with a configured packet rate of 2pps,
and a default CoPP limit of 1000, packet drops will be observed. In such instances the CoPP limit should be
increased to more than 2000.

Public

Configuration recommendations 328

The  show copp statistics class ptp  command can be used to monitor whether the CoPP
policy must be adjusted. For example:

Statistics for CoPP policy 'default':

Class: ptp

Description: Precision Time Protocol (PTP) .

    priority             : 5

    rate (pps)           : 1000

    burst size (pkts)    : 250

    packets passed   : 611153             packets dropped  : 0

NOTE

Increasing the PTP CoPP values may help in scaling the clients but needs to be
planned and configured properly so as to not to impact other protocols.

QoS prioritization configuration recommendations for a transparent clock

class ip PTP

    10 match udp any any eq 319 count

policy PTP-POL

    10 class ip PTP action local-priority 6

policy test

interface lag 240

    apply policy PTP-POL in

interface 1/1/26

    apply policy PTP-POL in

interface 2/1/26

    apply policy PTP-POL in

NOTE

PTP Event messages carrying a critical timestamp use UDP port 319.

General guidelines for PTP IPv4 multicast

•  For IP multicast-based PTP time distribution, it is recommended to use PIM Sparse-Mode.

•  When connecting transparent clock (TC) and boundary clock (BC), ensure that the TC becomes the DR

by setting the DR priority.

•  Ensure the mroutes are programmed on TCs so that there is reachability for PTP streams from the

upstream.

•  Configure static-igmp groups on TCs if the clients themselves cannot send IGMP joins for the PTP

multicast group.

Public

Configuration recommendations 329

•  The  QoS trust dscp  command needs to be explicitly configured on all non-BC switches in the

network to ensure that the incoming DSCP value of PTP traffic is honored.

•

If Peer-to-Peer (P2P) does not happen with the immediate peer (for example some configurations with
Unicast mode that skip over intermediate-nodes, which could be one or more Transparent Clocks) the
offsets may go high.

•  Transparent clock peer-to-peer using IPv4 transport requires source IP configuration to send IPv4 PTP

messages over L2 MCLAG.

Expected behavior of PTP with VSX (boundary clock)

Procedure

1.  PTP global disable or interface-level configuration events - Disabling PTP globally on one of the VSX

devices may result in increased offset values, depending on the PTP packet exchange path. The affected
device operates as a non-PTP-aware node, which can lead to higher offsets or loss of synchronization
until PTP is re-enabled.

2.  PTP interface disable/enable on MCLAG interfaces - When PTP is disabled on an MCLAG interface of
one VSX device, the PTP daemon stops processing PTP packets. As a result, the handling of multicast
PTP packets (L2 or L3) depends on the underlying multicast behavior. The device effectively becomes
PTP-unaware, potentially causing offset variations in downstream clients. Normal synchronization
resumes once the PTP configuration is re-enabled.

3.  PTP configuration mismatch between VSX peers - If the PTP configurations between VSX peers are

inconsistent (e.g., differences in profile, clock-step, clock-mode, or transport-mode), the system behavior
can become unpredictable. Each device may process PTP packets differently, leading to unstable offsets
and inconsistent synchronization across the network.

4.  VSX ISL flap events (ISL shut/unshut) - When the VSX ISL is shut, offset values typically remain stable,
with only minor spikes lasting a few seconds depending on the PTP tree convergence. After unshutting
the ISL and restoring MCLAG connectivity, brief offset spikes may occur during PTP reconvergence
between VSX devices. These stabilize shortly thereafter.

5.  VSX keepalive (KA) flap events (with ISL down and split-recovery disabled) - During a VSX Keepalive
flap with the ISL down and split-recovery disabled, downstream PTP clients generally maintain stable
offsets. However, the VSX Secondary device becomes unsynchronized from the Grandmaster (GM) and
operates as a standalone PTP device, affecting the synchronization of clients connected to it.

6.  VSX software upgrade events - When both VSX devices are synchronized with the Grandmaster,
performing a software upgrade using the vsx-swupdate command exhibits the following behavior:

a.  During secondary reboot: Downstream client offsets remain stable or experience minor spikes that

settle within a few seconds.

Public

Configuration recommendations 330

b.  During primary reboot: Downstream client offsets remain largely unaffected or show minimal spikes,

which stabilize quickly.

c.  Post-reboot and link restoration: Once the Primary device and MCLAGs are operational, brief offset

spikes may occur during PTP reconvergence, similar to the ISL recovery scenario.

7.  VSX common system MAC configuration - Configuring a common system MAC on VSX may

momentarily cause an offset spike due to an ISL flap. Despite this, MCLAGs remain operational even
when the ISL is down. The offset stabilizes once the VSX Secondary shuts its MCLAGs.

Unsupported/not-Supported PTP and VSX configurations

Both VSX devices must have identical PTP configurations, including PTP clock-mode, PTP clock-step, PTP
transport-mode, and PTP profile. If the PTP configurations between the VSX peers differ, the PTP+VSX
functionality will not operate as intended. The PTP daemon will verify if the peer VSX device has the same
PTP clock-mode (BC).

In a negative scenario, disabling PTP on one VSX device (which was previously receiving PTP Announce and
Sync packets from the upstream) while keeping it enabled on the other can lead to unpredictable behavior.
However to ensure that the VSX secondary device receives and processes upstream packets through a
PTP-unaware VSX primary device, the ISL PTP VLAN must match the upstream PTP VLAN. If the ISL PTP
VLAN does not align with the upstream PTP VLAN, the VSX secondary device will drop the received PTP
packets without processing them.

PTP on VSX keepalive interface

PTP configuration on keepalive interfaces are recommended only on non-OOBM interfaces. PTP is not
supported on OOBM interfaces as they do not allow PTP configurations. PTP on keepalive remains disabled
even though PTP is configured on KA as long as VSX ISL is active and in sync with VSX Peer. PTP on
keepalive will be enabled only when the VSX ISL interface is disabled and remains in shutdown state.

Use cases

NOTE
The following use cases are only supported on the 8360 Switch Series.

Select a use case from the list in the left navigation menu.

Subtopics

Use case: PTP – IPv4 over L2 spine leaf topology
Use case: PTP – L3 spine leaf topology
Use case: PTP-VSX Multicast
Use case: PTP TC VSX, BC VSF Topology connected via MCLAG

Public

Use cases 331

Use case: PTP – IPv4 over L2 spine leaf topology

Figure 1. Grand clock source connected to boundary clock (spines), followed by transparent
clock leaves

The following considerations and best practices apply to this use case illustrated in the above topology:

•  Configure candidate-RP on switches that are connected to a grand clock source. The candidate-RP

needs to be configured on the BC1 Spine A and BC2 Spine B.

•  When the PTP clients are not capable of sending IGMP joins, be sure to configure ip igmp static-group

224.0.1.129 on the VLAN interface of a TC, where PTP is configured.

NOTE
In this case, it is an L2 switch in leaf so  ip igmp snooping enable  also
needs to be configured on the transparent clock VLAN interface.

•  Ensure that ip source-interface ptp is configured on both BC switches on the VLAN interface where

PTP is configured.

•  Ensure that spanning tree is enabled to avoid unwanted loops.

•

In this use case topology, It is recommended to have VRRP on boundary clock switches to have L3
redundancy for PTP end clients.

Use case: PTP – L3 spine leaf topology

Public

Use case: PTP – IPv4 over L2 spine leaf topology 332

Figure 1. Grand clock source connected to transparent clock, followed by boundary clock spines
and boundary clock leaves

The following considerations and best practices apply to this use case illustrated in the above topology:

•  The number of PTP VLANs that can be configured per port is limited to 8.

•  Configure candidate-RP on switches which are connected to a grand clock source. Candidate-RP needs
to be configured on TC1 and TC2. Each candidate-RP controls multicast traffic forwarding for one or
more multicast groups.

•  Configure higher DR priority on switches in which candidate-RP is configured. The DR priority needs to

be configured on the TC1 and TC2 on the BC facing ports.

•  When the PTP clients are not capable of sending IGMP joins, ensure that ip igmp static-group

224.0.1.129 is configured on all PTP-enabled interfaces of a TC.

Public

Use case: PTP – L3 spine leaf topology 333

•  The igmp static-group configuration is not needed on BC switches. In this case, it is an L3 network so ip

igmp enable also needs to be configured on all PTP-enabled L3 interfaces of the TC.

Use case: PTP-VSX Multicast

Figure 1. Medium or Large campus solutions for PTP-VSX multicast

Public

Use case: PTP-VSX Multicast 334

The following considerations and best practices apply to the use case illustrated in the above topology.

Public

Use case: PTP-VSX Multicast 335

•

If there are no dynamic IGMP joins for the PTP Group on the Grand Master Clock (GMC)/client-
connected port on the core Boundary Clock (BC), use the command igmp static group to configure an
IGMP static groups on those ports.

•  When a core switch is acting as a Boundary Clock, it is always recommended to use Multi-Chassis Link

Aggregation (MC-LAG) with active forwarding as an upstream solution.

NOTE

In the above topology, if there is a requirement to use ECMP upstream solutions
in VSX, the upstream switch should be configured as Transparent Clock (TC).

Use case: PTP TC VSX, BC VSF Topology connected via MCLAG

About this task

This recommendation is to address deployments where multicast config change is not feasible and with the
help of ACLs unwanted PTP multicast packets can be avoided hitting the PTP BC and overwhelming the
PTP CoPP limit.

VSX TC and VSF topology with clients on VSX and VSF

Public

Use case: PTP TC VSX, BC VSF Topology connected vi... 336

U
s
e

c
a
s
e
:

P
T
P

T
C

V
S
X
,

B
C

V
S
F

T
o
p
o
l
o
g
y

c
o
n
n
e
c
t
e
d

v
i
.
.
.

Procedure

1.

In the above sample topology, it is possible that multicast PTP delay request from the PTP clients
connected to VSX TC which is intended only to the GM may get replicated to the downstream VSF PTP
BC as well if the general multicast routing is configured in such fashion.

2.

If there are multiple clients on the VSX layer and those clients are in different VLANs, then it may create
a multicast storm of PTP messages reaching the BC.

3.  To protect the CPU from being overwhelmed, the PTP BC's COPP module will start dropping the

excessive PTP messages.

4.  The CoPP drops on the BC would cause Announce/Sync/Delay-resp messages from the GM also to get

dropped and may result in loss of Synchronization of BC and its PTP clients.

5.

6.

In such cases, it is recommended to change the IP multicast configuration to avoid these unwanted PTP
packets getting forwarded to the BC. Alternatively, configure ACLs to drop the packets reaching the BC.

If the configuration changes listed above are not feasible then alternatively the CoPP limit for PTP
can be increased from the default CoPP value after considering the CoPP resource utilization of other
features configured in the switch.

Results

Sample ACL configuration

In VSF BC, igmp snooping ACLs can be configured to avoid advertisement of downstream IGMP VLANs
towards the VSX.

   access‐list ip ptp_control
    10 deny any 224.0.1.129 any count

    20 permit any any any

    vlan 52

        ip igmp snooping enable

    vlan 53

        ip igmp snooping enable
        ip igmp snooping apply access‐list ptp_control
In the VSX TC, ACLs can be applied on all the multicast source destination PTP clients to be dropped on the
MCLAG.

  access‐list ip ptp_control
    8 deny any 49.49.49.0/255.255.0.0 224.0.1.129

    9 deny any 51.51.51.0/255.255.0.0 224.0.1.129
    10 deny any 52.52.52.0/255.255.0.0 224.0.1.129

    20 deny any 53.53.53.0/255.255.0.0 224.0.1.129

    100 permit any any any

Public

Use case: PTP TC VSX, BC VSF Topology connected vi... 337

interface lag 1 multi‐chassis
  apply access‐list ip ptp_control out

NOTE

The ACLs and multicast configuration recommendations are only for reference
and may differ for different deployments. Behavior is not specific to VSF and VSX
topology and can be seen in any other topology as well as wih standalone BC
switches.

clear ptp statistics

Syntax

clear ptp statisctics [<IFNAME>]

Description

Clears PTP counters for the given interface.

Parameter

Description

Optional: Specifies the interface name.

<IFNAME>

Examples

Clearing PTP counters for the given interface:

switch# clear ptp statistics 1/1/8

switch# clear ptp statistics lag1

switch# clear ptp statistics

Command Information

Platforms

Command context

Authority

Manager ( # )

ts for this command.

8325P

8360

clock-domain

Syntax

clock-domain <DOMAIN-NUMBER>

no clock-domain <DOMAIN-NUMBER>

Description

Configures the PTP clock domain to a specified value.

The no form of this command removes the PTP domain configuration of the PTP clock.

Parameter

Description

Sets the PTP clock domain. Range: 0 to 254. Value configurable
subject to limits established by the PTP profile.

<DOMAIN‐NUMBER>

Usage

•  The one-step end-to-end transparent clock works across domains.

•  For boundary clocks, the clock-domain has to be identical with the domain used in the network.

•  All PTP devices must be within same domain to be able to sync with each other.

•  This command is only enabled in the PTP profile context.

•  For PTP transparent clock, you must configure the same clock-domain as on clients and GM to

synchronize.

Public

clock-domain 340

Examples

Entering the PTP profile context and setting the PTP clock domain value:

switch(config)# ptp profile 1588v2

switch(config-ptp)#clock-domain 4

Removing the PTP clock domain value:

switch(config-ptp)# no clock-domain

clock-step

Syntax

clock-step {one-step|two-step}

no clock-step {one-step|two-step}

Description

Configures the clock step mode that determines when the egress-time information is sent.

The 8325P and 9300 Switch Series only support the one-step command.

Public

clock-step 341

The 8360 Switch Series supports both one-step and two-step modes for boundary clocks. For transparent
clocks, only one-step mode is supported.

The no form of this command removes the PTP clock-step configuration of the PTP clock.

Parameter

Description

one‐step

two‐step

Sets the PTP clock‐step mode to one‐step messaging in whi
ch egress‐time information is sent along with the SYNC messa
ge.

Sets the PTP clock‐step mode to two‐step messaging in wh
ich egress‐time information is sent a subsequent follow‐up
message with the egress timestamp of the previously sent SYN
C message.

Usage

•  Mandatory command to start the PTP clock.

•  Boundary clocks can inter-operate with different step modes upstream or downstream.

Example

Setting the clock-step mode to one-step messaging:

switch(config-ptp)# clock-step one-step

Removing the clock-step mode configuration:

switch(config-ptp)# no clock-step

Setting the clock-step mode to two-step messaging:

switch(config-ptp)# clock-step two-step

enable

Syntax

enable

no enable

Description

Enables the PTP profile globally. However, the PTP clock is started only when all the mandatory commands
are set.

The no form of this command disables the PTP profile globally.

Usage

Mandatory command to start the PTP clock.

Examples

Enabling the PTP profile:

switch(config)# ptp profile 1588v2

switch(config-ptp)# enable

Disabling the PTP profile:

switch(config)# ptp profile 1588v2

switch(config-ptp)# no enable

config‐ptp

Administrators or local user group members with execution righ
ts for this command.

8325P

8360

9300

ip source-interface

Syntax

ip source-interface {ptp | all} interface <IFNAME> [vrf <VRF-NAME>

ip source-interface {ptp | all} <IPV4-ADDR> [vrf <VRF-NAME>]

no ip source-interface {ptp | all} interface <IFNAME> [vrf <VRF-NAME>

no ip source-interface {ptp | all} <IPV4-ADDR> [vrf <VRF-NAME>]

Description

Configures the source IP address to be used when sending PTP messages. Use the  ptp  keyword to set
source IP address specific to the PTP feature. If the feature-specific configuration is not available, the source
IP address corresponding to the  all  option will be used.

The  no  form of this command removes the configuration of the source IP address used by the PTP feature.

Parameter

Description

ptp

all

Selects the PTP protocol.

Selects all protocols that can be configured by this command.

Public

ip source-interface 344

Parameter

Description

interface <IF‐NAME>

Specifies the name of the interface from which the source IP ad
dress is obtained. The interface must have a valid IP address as
signed to it.

If the interface has both a primary and secondary IP address, th
e primary IP address is used.

vrf <VRF‐NAME>

Specifies the VRF name.

Specifies the source IPv4 address to be used.

The IP address must be defined on the switch, and it must exist
on the specified VRF (which is the default VRF, if the vrf option
is not used). Specify the address in IPv4 format (x.x.x.x), where
x is a decimal number from 0 to 255.

<IPV4‐ADDR>

Usage

•  This command must be configured on the switch when PTP is enabled on VLAN trunk or access ports,

and the transport protocol is IPv4.

•

In the current version of PTP, only the default VRF is supported.

•  This command is not applicable to the end-to-end transparent clock.

Examples

Configuring the source IP address for sending PTP messages:

switch(config)# ip source-interface ptp interface 1/1/1

switch(config)# ip source-interface ptp 10.10.10.1

switch(config)# ip source-interface ptp interface vlan10

10.08

ip source-interface 345

config

Administrators or local user group members with execution righ
ts for this command.

8325P

8360

9300

mode (PTP)

Syntax

mode boundary {end-to-end | peer-to-peer}

no mode boundary {end-to-end | peer-to-peer}

mode transparent {end-to-end | peer-to-peer}

no mode transparent {end-to-end | peer-to-peer}

Description

Configures the switch PTP clock mode, either boundary or transparent, with a delay-request mechanism
of either end-to-end or peer-to-peer. A device in transparent clock mode does not synchronize (syntonize)
itself to a grandsource clock. The 9300 and 8325P Switch Series support the mode boundary end-to-end
command only.

The no form of this command unconfigures the PTP clock mode and delay-request mechanism.

Parameter

Description

boundary

Selects boundary clock mode.

transparent

Selects transparent clock mode.

end‐to‐
end

Selects the end‐to‐end delay‐request mechanism.

peer‐to‐
peer

Selects the peer‐to‐peer delay‐request mechanism. Not su
pported with VSF.

Examples

Configuring PTP boundary clock mode with the end-to-end delay-request mechanism:

Public

mode (PTP) 346

switch(config-ptp)# mode boundary end-to-end

Unconfiguring PTP boundary clock mode with the end-to-end delay-request mechanism:

switch(config-ptp)# no mode boundary end-to-end

Configuring PTP boundary clock mode with the peer-to-peer delay-request mechanism:

switch(config-ptp)# mode boundary peer-to-peer

Unconfiguring PTP boundary clock mode with the peer-to-peer delay-request mechanism:

switch(config-ptp)# no mode boundary peer-to-peer

Configuring PTP transparent with the end-to-end delay-request mechanism:

switch(config-ptp)# mode transparent end-to-end

Unconfiguring PTP transparent with the end-to-end delay-request mechanism:

switch(config-ptp)# no mode transparent end-to-end

Configuring PTP transparent with the peer-to-peer delay-request mechanism:

switch(config-ptp)# mode transparent peer-to-peer

Unconfiguring PTP transparent with the peer-to-peer delay-request mechanism:

switch(config-ptp)# no mode transparent peer-to-peer

8325P

multicast-address

Syntax

multicast-address {forwarding|non-forwarding}

no multicast-address {forwarding|non-forwarding}

Description

Sets the multicast Ethernet MAC address used for PTP packets to be forwardable (01:1B:19:00:00:00) or
non-forwardable (01:80:C2:00:00:0E) per the ITU G.8275.1 standard.

The no form of this command removes the PTP domain configuration of the PTP clock.

NOTE

This command is only available for the G.8275.1 profile.

Parameter

forwarding

Description

Selects forwardable multicast MAC addresses for PTP. Default.

non‐forwarding

Selects non‐forwardable multicast MAC addresses for PTP.

Examples

Selecting forwardable MAC addresses for PTP:

switch(config)# ptp profile g.8275.1
switch(config-ptp)# multicast-address forwarding

Selecting non- forwardable MAC addresses for PTP:

Public

multicast-address 348

switch(config)# ptp profile g.8275.1

switch(config-ptp)# multicast-address non-forwarding

Unconfiguring the multicast ethernet address setting used for PTP packets. This will set the multicast
address state to forwarding (the default state):

switch(config)# ptp profile g.8275.1

switch(config-ptp)# no multicast-address

10.15

Modification

Command introduced.

Command Information

Platforms

Command context

Authority

8325P

config‐ptp

Administrators or local user group members with execution righ
ts for this command.

priority1

Syntax

priority1 <PRIORITY>

no priority1 <PRIORITY>

Description

Configures the PTP clock priority1 value of the device. This value is operational when the device is in
boundary clock mode and participating in the Best Clock Source Algorithm (BMCA). This value is used to
indicate priority to its downstream clock-aware devices.

NOTE
Priority 1 is fixed as 128 on the 8325P when using the G.8275.1 or G.8275.2
profile and is not configurable.

Public

priority1 349

The no form of this command removes the PTP priority1 configuration of the PTP clock and sets it to the
default value of 128.

Parameter

Description

Sets the priority value. Default 128.

<PRIORITY>

Usage

This value can be configured only for the boundary clock.

Examples

Configuring PTP priority1 value:

switch(config-ptp)# priority1 129

Removing PTP priority1 configuration:

switch(config-ptp)# no priority1

ptp announce-interval

Syntax

ptp announce-interval {1588v2 | g.8275.2 | aes67 | aes-r16 | dot1as |

smpte} <LOG-SECONDS>

no ptp announce-interval {1588v2| g.8275.2 | aes67 | aes-r16 | dot1as |

smpte}

Description

Sets the announce message transmit interval on a PTP-enabled interface for a specific PTP profile.

The no form of this command removes the announce message transmit interval configuration on a PTP-
enabled interface and sets a profile specific default value.

NOTE

The g.8275.1 profile doesn't support announce-interval configuration. Its value is
fixed to -3.

Parameter

Description

1588v2

Specifies the PTP 1588v2 profile timers. Range: ‐6 to 1. Defa
ult: 1.

Public

ptp announce-interval 352

Parameter

g.8275.2

Description

Specifies the PTP g.8275.2 profile timers. Range: ‐3 to 0. De
fault: 0.

aes67

Specifies the PTP AES67 profile timers. Range: 0 to 1. Default:
1.

aes‐r16

dot1as

Specifies the PTP AES‐R16 profile timers. Range: 0 to 1. Defa
ult: 1.

Specifies the PTP 802.1AS profile timers. Range: ‐6 to 1. Defa
ult: 0.

smpte

Specifies the PTP SMTPE profile timers. Range: ‐3 to 1. Defau
lt: 0.

Sets the announce message interval in log seconds.

<LOG‐SECONDS>

Switch series

Supported profiles

8325P

8360

9300‐32D

1588v2, g.8275.2 , smpte

1588v2, dot1as, aes67, aes‐r16, smpte

This value can be configured only for the boundary clock.

ptp clock-source-only

Syntax

ptp clock-source-only

no ptp clock-source-only

Public

ptp clock-source-only 356

Description

Configures the PTP port state to clock_source state. This prohibits the port from entering into a clock_sink or
passive state.

The no form of this command removes the clock_source state configuration on the port and returns it to
normal BMCA operation.

Usage

This can only be configured for the boundary clock.

Examples

Configuring the clock_source only role for the port:

switch(config)# interface 1/1/1

switch(config-if)# ptp clock-source-only

Removing the configuration of clock_source only role for the port:

switch(config)# interface 1/1/1

switch(config-if)# no ptp clock-source-only

The no form of this command removes the delay_req message transmit interval configuration on a PTP-
enabled interface and sets a profile specific default value.

ult 0.

fault 0.

t 0.

aes‐r16

ult 0.

smpte

Specifies the PTP SMTPE profile timers. ‐7 to 1. Default ‐3.

Sets the delay_req message interval in log seconds.

<LOG‐SECONDS>

8325P

ptp delay-req-interval 358

8360

9300‐32D

mode.

Setting the PTP 1588v2 profile timers:

switch(config-if)# no ptp delay-req-interval 1588v2

ptp enable

Syntax

ptp enable

no ptp enable

Description

Enables PTP on the interface. The no form of this command disables PTP on the interface. PTP can be
enabled only on physical L2 or L3 interfaces and LAG L2 or L3 interfaces.

Examples

Enabling PTP on a physical interface:

switch(config)# interface 1/1/1

switch(config-if)# ptp enable

Disabling PTP on the interface context:

switch(config)# interface 1/1/1

switch(config-if)# no ptp enable

Public

ptp enable 360

ptp lag-role

Syntax

ptp lag-role {primary | secondary}

no ptp lag-role {primary | secondary}

Description

Configures the PTP role for the member interfaces of a Link Aggregation (LAG) . When there are two or
more member interfaces for a LAG, only one link can be configured as primary and only one other link can be
configured as secondary. The primary member interface is used for transmitting the PTP packets generated
by the boundary clock. When the primary member goes down, the secondary member is used for PTP packet
transmission. If both primary and secondary members go down, PTP does not flip over to the other links of
the LAG.

The no form of this command removes the PTP role configuration for the LAG member interface.

NOTE

This command is not supported when configured as a transparent clock.

Parameter

Description

primary

Sets the primary PTP lag‐role for the LAG member interface.

secondary

Sets the secondary PTP lag‐role for the LAG member interfac
e.

Usage

•  LAG roles must be configured for boundary clock.

•  For the primary or secondary LAG roles, ensure that the same link ports are configured on both ends of

the LAG.

Examples

Setting the primary PTP lag-role for the LAG member interface:

switch(config)# interface 1/1/1
switch(config-if)# ptp lag-role primary

Setting the secondary PTP lag-role for the LAG member interface:

Public

ptp lag-role 361

switch(config)# interface 1/1/2

switch(config-if)# ptp lag-role secondary

Removing the PTP lag-role configuration for the LAG member interface:

switch(config)# interface 1/1/1

switch(config-if)# no ptp lag-role

onds. The supported range is 0‐2147483648.

ptp peer ip

Public

ptp peer ip 365

Syntax
ptp peer ip <IP-ADDRESS>
no ptp peer ip <IP-ADDRESS>
Description
Configures destination IP addresses for the interfaces in unicast transmission.
The no form of this command removes the PTP destination IP address configuration for the interfaces in
unicast transmission.
NOTE
Unicast and multicast configurations are mutually exclusive. If a unicast port is
configured, all multicast ports will cease operating. If multicast transmission is
desired no unicast ports can be configured.
Parameter Description
ip <IP‐ Specifies the peer IPv4 address. Syntax: A.B.C.D
ADDRESS>
Usage
•  This command has no effect when configured as a transparent clock.
Example
| Configuring  | ptp peer ip |  on the interface: |     |     |
| ------------ | ----------- | ------------------ | --- | --- |
switch(config)# interface 1/1/1
switch(config-if)# ptp peer ip 10.0.0.1

| Removing  |     |  on the interface: |     |     |
| --------- | --- | ------------------ | --- | --- |
ptp peer ip
switch(config-if)# no ptp peer ip 10.0.0.1

8325P

8360

ptp peer-to-peer vlan

Syntax

ptp peer-to-peer vlan <VLAN-ID>

no ptp peer-to-peer vlan

Description

Configures a VLAN for PTP pdelay messages.

The no form of this command removes the VLAN configuration for PTP pdelay messages on a specified port.

NOTE

According to Telco standard, the insertion of a VLAN tag is not supported on
G.8275.1 profiles. Also, the port will be disabled for PTP if the PTP VLAN is
misconfigured.

Parameter

Description

Specifies VLAN as single VLAN ID. Range 1‐4094.

<VLAN‐ID>

Public

ptp peer-to-peer vlan 367

Usage

•  This configuration has no bearing on only one-step transparent clock end-to-end, but will be configured

on peer-to-peer mode.

•

In boundary peer-to-peer clock mode, only PTP pdelay packets with PTP peer-to-peer VLAN are
processed. PTP pdelay packets from other VLANs are dropped.

•  ptp peer-to-peer vlan should be configured on interfaces only when the specific VLAN is a trunk/

tagged member of that port. This configuration should not be performed on an access port.

Examples

Configuring VLAN 4 for PTP pdelay messages on interface 1/1/1:

switch(config)# interface 1/1/1

switch(config-if)# ptp peer-to-peer vlan 4

Removing the VLAN configuration for PTP messages:

switch(config)# interface 1/1/1

switch(config-if)# no ptp peer-to-peer vlan

Command History

Release

10.17

Modification

Command introduced.

Command Information

Platforms

Command context

Authority

8360

config‐if

Administrators or local user group members with execution righ
ts for this command.

ptp profile

Public

ptp profile 368

Syntax

ptp profile {<PROFILE NAME>}

no ptp profile {<PROFILE NAME>}

Description

Enters the PTP context to configure the PTP profile in which the device will operate. Configure a PTP profile
before configuring mode or other profile-specific parameters. The device can be operating in any one profile
at a given point of time.

The no form of this command removes the PTP profile configuration in which the device will operate. This
command clears the PTP profile and all parameters related to that profile.

Parameter

<PROFILE

NAME>

Description

Specifies the profile to be used. Profiles include:

•  1588v2: Specifies the IEEE 1588‐2008 profile to be used.
•  aes67: Specifies the IEEE AES67 profile to be used.
•  aes‐r16: Specifies the IEEE AES‐R16‐2016 profile to

be used.

•  dot1as: Specifies the IEEE 802.1AS profile to be used.
•  g.8275.1: Specifies the ITU G.8275.1 profile to be used.
•  g.8275.2: Specifies the ITU G.8275.2 profile to be used.
•  smpte: Specifies the IEEE SMPTE‐ST‐2059‐2 profile

to be used.

Switch series

Supported profiles

8325P

8360

9300‐32D

Usage

1588v2, g.8275.1, g.8275.2 , smpte

1588v2, aes‐r16, aes67, dot1as, smpte

1588v2, smpte

Configure PTP profile before configuring mode or other profile-specific parameters.

Example

Configuring PTP profile 1588v2:

switch(config)# ptp profile 1588v2

Public

ptp profile 369

Configuring PTP profile smpte:

switch(config)# ptp profile smpte

Configuring more than one PTP profile:

switch(config)# ptp profile 1588v2

switch(config-ptp)# exit

switch(config)# ptp profile smpte

switch(config-ptp)#

The existing profile must be removed using the 'no ptp profile' command

before configuring a different profile.
Removing the PTP profile:

switch(config-ptp)# no ptp profile

ptp sync-interval

Public

ptp sync-interval 370

Syntax

ptp sync-interval {1588v2 | g.8275.2 | dot1as | aes67 | smpte} <LOG-SECONDS>

no ptp sync-interval {1588v2 | g.8275.2 | dot1as | aes67 | smpte}

Description

Sets the sync message transmit interval on a PTP-enabled interface for a specific PTP profile.

The no form of this command removes the sync message transmit interval configuration on a PTP enabled
interface and sets it to a profile-specific default value.

NOTE

The g.8275.1 profile doesn't support sync-interval configuration. Its value is fixed
to -4.

Parameter

Description

1588v2

Specifies the PTP 1588v2 profile timers. Range: ‐6 to 1. Defa
ult 0.

g.8275.2

dot1as

Specifies the PTP g.8275.2 profile timers. Range: ‐6 to 1. De
fault 0.

Specifies the PTP 802.1 AS profile timers. Range: ‐6 to 1. De
fault ‐3.

aes67

Specifies the PTP AES67 profile timers. Range: ‐4 to 1. Defaul
t ‐3.

smpte

Specifies the PTP SMTPE profile timers. Range: ‐7 to ‐1. De
fault ‐3

Sets the sync message interval in log seconds.

<LOG‐SECONDS>

Switch series

Supported profiles

8325P

8360

9300‐32D

1588v2, g.8275.2 , smpte

1588v2, dot1as, aes67, smpte

1588v2, smpte

Public

ptp sync-interval 371

Examples

Setting the PTP SMPTE sync interval :

switch(config)# interface 1/1/1

switch(config-if)# ptp sync-interval smpte -2

Setting the PTP 1588v2 sync interval :

switch(config)# interface 1/1/1

switch(config-if)# ptp sync-interval 1588v2 -3

Setting the PTP AES67 sync interval :

switch(config)# interface 1/1/1

switch(config-if)# ptp sync-interval aes67 -2

Removing the PTP AES67 sync interval:

switch(config)# interface 1/1/1

switch(config-if)# no ptp sync-interval aes67

ptp sync-interval 372

ptp sync-timeout

Syntax

ptp sync-timeout dot1as <COUNT>

no ptp sync-timeout dot1as

ptp sync-timeout {g.8275.1|g.8275.2} <COUNT>

no ptp sync-timeout {g.8275.1|g.8275.2}

Description

Sets the synchronization message receipt timeout on a PTP enabled interface.

The no form of this command resets the synchronization message receipt timeout configuration on a
PTP-enabled interface and sets a profile-specific default value.

NOTE

The dot1as profile is supported on the 8360. The g.8275.1 and g.8275.2 profiles
are supported on the 8325P.

Parameter

dot1as

<COUNT>

g.8275.1

g.8275.2

Examples

Description

Specifies the PTP 802.1AS profile timers.

Specifies the sync timeout count.

6300 and 8360: Range: 2‐255. Default: 3.

8325P: Range: 0‐255. Default: 5.

Specifies the PTP G.8275.1 profile timers.

Specifies the PTP G.8275.1 profile timers.

Setting the PTP 802.1 AS profile timer:

switch(config)# interface 1/1/1

switch(config-if)# ptp sync-timeout dot1as 4

Resetting the PTP 802.1 AS profile timer:

switch(config)# interface 1/1/1

switch(config-if)# no ptp sync-timeout dot1as

Public

ptp sync-timeout 373

Setting the PTP g.8275.1 profile timer:

switch(config)# interface 1/1/1

switch(config-if)# ptp sync-timeout g.8275.1 6

Resetting the PTP g.8275.1 profile timer:

switch(config)# interface 1/1/1

switch(config-if)# no ptp sync-timeout g.8275.1

Command introduced.

Command Information

Platforms

Command context

Authority

8325P

8360

config‐if

Administrators or local user group members with execution righ
ts for this command.

ptp vlan

Syntax

ptp vlan <VLAN-ID>

no ptp vlan <VLAN-ID>

Description

Configures a VLAN for PTP messages. It is necessary when the boundary clock port is a VLAN trunk L2
interface (no routing).

Public

ptp vlan 374

The no form of this command removes the VLAN configuration for PTP messages.

NOTE

According to Telco standard, the insertion of a VLAN tag is not supported on
G.8275.1 profiles. Also, the port will be disabled for PTP if the PTP VLAN is
misconfigured.

Parameter

Description

For the 8325P, 8325H, and 9300 Switch Series: Specifies a VLA
N. Range: 1‐4094.

For the 8360 Switch Series: Specifies VLAN as single VLAN ID,
range or list. Range: 1‐4094.

<VLAN‐ID>

Usage

•  This configuration has no bearing on only one-step transparent clock end-to-end. Ptp peer-to-peer

vlan will be configured on transparent clock peer-to-peer mode.

•

In boundary clock mode, only PTP packets in PTP VLAN are processed; PTP packets from other VLANs
are dropped.

•  ptp vlan should be configured on interfaces only when the specific VLAN is a trunk/tagged member of

that port. This configuration should not be performed on an access port.

Examples

Configuring VLANs 4 and 5 for PTP messages:

switch(config)# interface 1/1/1

switch(config-if)# ptp vlan 4,5

Configuring VLANs 2 through 5 for PTP messages:

switch(config)# interface 1/1/1

switch(config-if)# ptp vlan 2-5

Removing the VLAN configuration for PTP messages:

switch(config)# interface 1/1/1

switch(config-if)# no ptp vlan

Public

ptp vlan 375

transport-protocol

Syntax

transport-protocol {ethernet | ip | udp <UDP-PORT>}

no transport-protocol

Description

Sets the transport protocol for PTP packets. In the case of IPv4, the UDP check-sum is reset. There is no
default transport-protocol.

The no form of this command disconnects the clock from its source.

Parameter

Description

ethernet

Specifies the Ethernet (Layer 2) transport protocol.

ip

Specifies the IPv4 transport protocol.

Public

transport-protocol 397

Parameter

Description

udp

Specifies the UDP transport protocol.

Specifies the transport layer port. Range: 1‐65355. The defaul
t is 4739.

<UDP‐PORT>

Usage

Mandatory command to start the PTP clock.

Example

Setting the Ethernet transport protocol for PTP packets:

switch(config-ptp)# transport-protocol ethernet

Removing the transport protocol for PTP packets:

switch(config-ptp)# no transport-protocol

Setting the transport protocol for exporter-1 to UDP with port 2055:

switch(config)# flow exporter exporter-1

switch(config-flow-exporter)# transport udp 2055

vsx-sync ptp-global

Syntax

vsx-sync ptp-global

no vsx-sync ptp-global

Description

Enables synchronization of Precision Time Protocol (PTP) configuration on the VSX peer switch.

The no form of this command disables the synchronization of PTP configuration on the VSX peer switch.
However, this will not delete the existing PTP configuration on the peer switch.

Examples

Enabling VSX synchronization for PTP configuration:

switch(config)# ptp profile 1588v2

switch(config-ptp)# mode transparent end-to-end

switch(config-ptp)# exit

switch(config)# vsx

switch(config-vsx)# vsx-sync ptp-global

Disabling VSX synchronization for PTP configuration:

switch(config)# vsx

switch(config-vsx)# no vsx-sync ptp-global

Public

vsx-sync ptp-global 399

SyncE commands

esmc enable

Syntax

esmc enable

no esmc enable

Public

SyncE commands 400

Description

Configures the interface as a Synchronous Ethernet port by enabling the ESMC protocol for this interface.

The interface is configured to behave as a clock source by default, meaning that it is able to receive ESMC
messages and will be considered in the SyncE clock selection process according to the ESMC protocol. The
interface will not transmit ESMC messages. Before enabling ESMC on an interface, it must be assigned a
unique priority. Refer to synce priority for additional SyncE clock source priority configuration information.

When an interface is configured to receive ESMC it will continuously monitor for the reception of ESMC
packets. The packets are expected at a rate of 1 per second (as specified in ITU-T G.8264). If the interface
stops receiving ESMC packets for more than 5 seconds, it will enter the "Rx timeout" condition and its
current quality level will be set to QL-FAILED.

The no form of the command disables the Synchronous Ethernet feature on an interface.

Examples

Enabling SyncE on an interface:

switch(config)# interface 1/1/1

switch(config-if)# esmc enable

Disabling SyncE on an interface:

switch(config)# interface 1/1/1

switch(config-if)# no esmc enable

Attempting to enable SyncE on an interface prior to configuring synce priority:

switch(config)# interface 1/1/1

switch(config-if)# esmc enable

Unique priority must be configured before enabling ESMC on interface

switch(config-if)# synce priority 1

switch(config-if)# esmc enable

Command History

10.15

Modification

Command introduced.

Public

esmc enable 401

config‐if

ts for this command.

esmc transmit-only

Syntax

esmc transmit-only

no esmc transmit-only

Description

Configures the interface as a Synchronous Ethernet transmit-only mode interface. This means that the
interface is not capable of receiving ESMC messages or running the ESMC protocol and will not be
considered in the clock selection process. Instead, the interface will send ESMC packets advertising the
current quality level of the system. Initial quality level cannot be configured on a transmit-only interface. The
synce priority can be configured but will be active only if the mode is rx/tx. When operating in tx-only mode,
the value will remain configured but inactive.

The no form of the command resets the interface to Synchronous Ethernet clock source mode. This means
that the interface will receive and process ESMC messages but will not transmit them. The interface will also
be eligible for the clock selection process.

NOTE

All SyncE-configured LAG member ports must be configured with same operation
mode, either transmit or receive. Mixing operation modes on different LAG
member ports is not supported.

Examples

Configuring interface 1/1/1 as transmit-only mode interface:

switch(config)# interface 1/1/1

switch(config-if)# esmc transmit-only

Resetting interface 1/1/1 to SyncE clock source mode:

switch(config)# interface 1/1/1

switch(config-if)# no esmc transmit-only

Public

esmc transmit-only 402

Configuring a priority on an interface:

switch(config)# interface 1/1/1

switch(config-if)# synce priority 2

Attempting to configure an initial quality level on an interface that has been configured as transmit-only:

switch(config)# interface 1/1/1

switch(config-if)# synce initial-quality-level PRC

Cannot configure initial quality level on transmit-only interface 1/1/1

show synce interface

Syntax

show synce interface [{<IFNAME> | brief}]

Description

Shows the current status of selected quality level, selected port name, selected clock index, SyncE DPLL
state, and holdover remaining time.

NOTE

The rank is a value used internally to select the best SyncE source. It is composed
as follows, in hexadecimal:

0x AA BB CC

AA = Quality level, BB = Priority, and CC = Number of hops. The lower the rank,
the better the source.

Public

show synce interface 403

Parameter

Description

Specifies an interface name.

<IFNAME>

brief

Examples

Specifies interface brief.

Showing an individual SyncE interface status:

switch# show synce interface 1/1/1

Status                      : Enabled

ESMC Mode                   : Receive

Clock State                 : Qualified

Sync State                  : Normal

Current Quality Level       : ePRTC

Priority                    : 11

Rank                        : 0x000B01

Number of Hops              : 1

Rx Status                   : Active
Showing the status of all interfaces with SyncE enabled:

Interface: 1/1/1

Sync State                  : Normal

ESMC Mode                   : Receive

Current Quality Level       : PRC

Configured Quality Level    : SSUA
Priority                    : 4

Rank                        : 0x031602

Number of Hops              : 2

Rx Status                   : Active

Interface: 1/1/2

Status                      : Enabled

ESMC Mode                   : Transmit

Interface: 1/1/12

Status                      : Enabled

ESMC Mode                   : Receive

Priority                    : Not set

show synce interface 404

Rx Status                   : Inactive

Interface 1/1/22:

Status                      : Enabled

ESMC Mode                   : Receive

Clock Index                 : 8

Clock State                 : Qualified

Sync State                  : Normal

Current Quality Level       : FAILED

Priority                    : 22

----------------------------------------------------------------

1/1/1        Clock       PRC               2            0x031602

1/1/4        Clock       NCFG              4            None

show synce status

Syntax

show synce status

Description

Displays the current status of selected quality level, selected port name, and the SyncE clock state. When the
selected clock source is the internal clock, the selected clock quality is the quality of the internal clock and
depends on the network type configured. When the SyncE service is not running such as when there is no
port with ESMC enabled or missing configuration, then the clock quality is shown as "N/A".

Network type to internal clock quality mapping:

•  Option 1 - QL-SEC

•  Option 2 - QL-SMC

Examples

Showing SyncE status where a port has been selected as the clock source and the local clock is synchronized
to its corresponding SyncE signal:

switch# show synce status

Synchronous Ethernet Status

-----------------------------

Selected Clock Quality    : QL-PRC

Selected Clock Source     : 1/1/1

Synchronization State:    : locked
Showing SyncE status when the connection to the selected clock was lost but the internal clock is within the
holdover period and with the SyncE default network type option 1:

switch# show synce status
Synchronous Ethernet Status

-----------------------------

Selected Clock Quality    : QL-SEC

Public

show synce status 406

Selected Clock Source     : Internal clock

Synchronization State     : holdover
Showing SyncE status with the SyncE service is running, no clock has been selected, and the SyncE default
network type option 1:

switch# show synce status

Synchronous Ethernet Status

-----------------------------

Selected Clock Quality    : N/A

Selected Clock Source     : Internal clock

Synchronization State     : freerun
Showing SyncE status with the SyncE service running, the synchronization has been lost, the holdover period
has expired, and the SyncE default network type option 1:

switch# show synce status

Synchronous Ethernet Status

-----------------------------

Selected Clock Quality    : QL-SEC

Selected Clock Source     : Internal clock

Synchronization State     : freerun
Showing SyncE status when the SyncE service is not running:

switch# show synce status

Synchronous Ethernet Status

-----------------------------

Selected Clock Quality    : N/A

Selected Clock Source     : Internal clock

Synchronization State     : freerun

ult.

synce network-type 410

synce priority

Syntax

synce priority <PRIORITY>

no synce priority [<PRIORITY>]

Description

Sets the priority of the Synchronous Ethernet clock source to use during selection. The priority is the main
criterion used for selection if the Quality Level is disabled, or if two clock sources have the same Quality
level.

The priority configuration must be unique and no two ports can have the same priority value.

Priority must be configured before enabling ESMC on an interface, unless the interface is configured in
transmit-only mode. Priority value 255 is reserved for the local oscillator, which is the selected clock when
the system is in holdover state.

The no form of this command removes the configured value of the priority for the Synchronous Ethernet
clock source and leaves the value as empty. If the SyncE feature was enabled on the interface it will remain
enabled, however the interface will not participate in the clock selection process.

NOTE

All SyncE-configured LAG member ports must be configured with same operation
mode, either transmit or receive. Mixing operation modes on different LAG
member ports is not supported.

Parameter

<PRIORITY>

Examples

Description

Specifies the priority for the Synchronous Ethernet Clock Sourc
e to be used in clock selection. Range: 0‐254.

Configuring interface 1/1/1 with a priority of 1:

switch(config)# interface 1/1/1

switch(config-if)# synce priority 1

Removing the configured priority 1 from interface 1/1/1:

switch(config)# interface 1/1/1

switch(config-if)# no synce priority 1

Public

synce priority 411

Command History

Release

10.15

Modification

Command introduced.

Platforms

Command context

Authority

8325P

config‐if

Administrators or local user group members with execution righ
ts for this command.

synce initial-quality-level

Syntax

synce initial-quality-level {ncfg|eprtc|prtc|eprc|prc|ssua|ssub|esec|sec|

dnu|prs|stu|st2|tnc|st3e|st3|smc|st4|prov|dus}

no synce initial-quality-level

[{ncfg|eprtc|prtc|eprc|prc|ssua|ssub|esec|sec|dnu|prs|stu|st2|tnc|st3e|st3|

smc|st4|prov|dus}]

Description

Configures the initial quality level of the clock source interface.

The initial quality level must be valid for the configured network-option, otherwise it will not be applied to
the interface. When an initial quality level better than DNU/DUS is configured, it is considered in the initial
calculation of rank and port selection. It will effectively be the current quality level of the interface until a
different quality level is received from ESMC or the "Rx timeout" condition is hit. If the interface is selected
as the best clock source during this transient period, then the switch will advertise this initial quality level as
the system current quality level, meaning that a device synchronized downstream will momentarily receive
this quality level. If this value is not configured, then the initial quality level for the interface during first rank
calculation will be QL-FAILED.

The no form of this command removes the configured initial quality level of the clock source interface.

Parameter

ncfg

Description

The quality level is not configured. Default.

Public

synce initial-quality-level 412

Parameter

eprtc

prtc

eprc

prc

ssua

ssub

esec

sec

dnu

prs

stu

st2

tnc

st3e

st3

smc

st4

prov

dus

Description

The timing quality of an enhanced primary reference time cloc
k. Can be configured on either network option‐1 or option‐2
.

The timing quality of a primary reference time clock. Can be co
nfigured on either network option‐1 or option‐2.

The timing quality of an enhanced primary reference clock. Can
be configured on either network option‐1 or option‐2.

Timing quality of a primary reference clock (option‐1 only).

Timing quality of a type I or IV clock (option‐1 only).

Timing quality of a type VI clock (option‐1 only).

The timing quality of an enhanced synchronous equipment cloc
k. Can be configured on network option‐1 only.

The timing quality of a synchronous equipment clock. Can be c
onfigured on network option‐1 only.

A clock source that should not be used for selection (option‐1
only).

Clock traceable to a primary reference source (option‐2 only).

Clock not traceable to an known source (option‐2 only).

Clock traceable to Stratum 2 (option‐2 only).

Clock traceable to a transit node clock (option‐2 only).

Clock traceable to Stratum 3E (option‐2 only).

Clock traceable to Stratum 3 (option‐2 only).

Clock traceable to a self‐timed SONET clock (option‐2 only).

Clock traceable to Stratum 4 free‐run (option‐2 only).

Clock source provisioned by the network operator. Can be confi
gured on network option‐2 only.

A clock source that should not be used for selection (option‐2
only).

Public

synce initial-quality-level 413

Examples

Configuring the initial quality level of the clock source interface as primary reference clock:

switch(config)# interface 1/1/1

switch(config-if)# synce initial-quality-level prc

Configuring the initial quality level of the clock source interface as a type I or IV clock:

switch(config)# interface 1/1/1

switch(config-if)# synce initial-quality-level ssua

Removing the configured initial quality level of the clock source interface:

switch(config)# interface 1/1/1

switch(config-if)# no synce initial-quality-level prs

Syntax

synce quality-mode

no synce quality-mode

Description

Enables Synchronous Ethernet clock selection based on the received quality level of clock sources. Enabled
by default.

Public

synce quality-mode 414

The no form of this command will ignore quality level when selecting a clock source. Selection will be based
on the configured priority.

Examples

Enabling SyncE clock selection based on the received quality level of clock sources:

switch(config)# synce quality-mode

Disabling Synchronous Ethernet clock selection based on quality level of clock sources:

switch(config)# no synce quality-mode

Public

synce wait-to-restore 415

Parameter

<SECONDS>

Examples

Description

Time in seconds that a Synchronous Ethernet port should rema
in fault‐free to be considered for clock selection. Range: 0‐7
20. Default: 60.

Setting the time for Synchronous Ethernet ports to be up before sending ESMC messages to 15 seconds:

switch(config)# synce wait-to-restore 15

Resetting the time for Synchronous Ethernet ports to be up before sending ESMC messages to the default
value of 60 seconds:

Command Information

Platforms

Command context

Authority

8325P

config

Administrators or local user group members with execution righ
ts for this command.

Audio-Video Bridging (AVB)

NOTE
AVB is supported only on the HPE Aruba Networking 6300 (R8S89A, R8S90A,
S0E91A, S0X44A, S4P41A, S4P42A, S4P43A, S4P44A, S4P45A, S4P46A,
S4P47A, and S4P48A) and 8360 Switch Series (JL717C, JL718C, JL721C, and
JL722C).

Public

Audio-Video Bridging (AVB) 416

Audio-Video Bridging (AVB) defines features, options, configurations, defaults, protocols, and procedures
of bridges, stations, and Local Area Networks (LANs) that are necessary to build networks that are
capable of transporting time-sensitive audio and/or video data streams. AVB operates on the concept of
stream reservation. Stream Reservation Protocol (SRP) is an amendment to the IEEE 802.1Q standard
(standardized separately as 802.1Qat) to provide end-to-end management of resource reservations for data
streams requiring guaranteed Quality of Service (QoS) in Local Area Networks (LANs).

AVB requires an advanced feature pack subscription for activation, and is supported only over the Common
and Internal SpanningTree (CIST).

Figure 1. Illustrative AVB topology with the validated profile

Multiple Stream Registration Protocol

Multiple Stream Registration Protocol (MSRP) is a signaling protocol that provides end-stations with the
ability to reserve network resources that guarantee the transmission and reception of data streams across
a bridged network with the requested QoS. The end stations are referred to as Talkers (devices that
produce data streams) and Listeners (devices that consume data streams). MSRP defines payloads and
rules for propagating registrations for both Talkers and Listeners. MSRP relies on Multiple Reservation
Protocol ( MRP) for its communication. MRP (IEEE 802.1Q-2018 section 10) is a robust, efficient protocol
for declaring attributes to be registered in a database in each port of each bridge (optionally, station) in a
bridged network.

Stream-ID should always be generated from Source MAC of talker and multicast AVB streams are supported.
MSRP rank-based prioritization and pre-emption is not supported. MSRP Listener registration is considered
when the corresponding talker stream is registered. Streams with non-AVB VLAN are sent as Talker failed
with error code 16 (Changes in FirstValue for a registered StreamID). As per protocol functionality, standard
failure codes 1, 5, 8, 14, 15, 16, and 19 are generated.

Subtopics

Public

Audio-Video Bridging (AVB) 417

MSRP timer configuration
QoS Configuration
AVB commands

MSRP timer configuration

Whenever MSRP timer config is changed, for the protocol to operate more efficiently, and with less likelihood
of unwanted deregistrations, the following relationships should be maintained between timer values used by
peer Participants:

•  LeaveTime should be at least twice the maximum JoinTime, plus six times the timer resolution, to allow

reregistration after a Leave or LeaveAll even if a message is lost.

•  To minimize the volume of rejoining traffic generated following a LeaveAll, the value chosen for

LeaveAllTime should be large relative to LeaveTime.

Forwarding and queueing for time sensitive streams

Forwarding and Queueing for Time-Sensitive Streams (FQTSS) is an IEEE 802.1Qav standard to describe a
set of procedures and tools that can be used to support the forwarding and queueing requirements for AVB.

QoS Configuration

AVB requires the Class-A and Class-B traffic to be given the highest priority and thus manages these
class-queues and a control-queue (reserved/future-use). While AVB manages the (3) AVB queues, flexibility
is available to customize the mapping of the local-priority to any (5) non-AVB queues. The following is one
of the suggested templates for Queue and Schedule profile:

Among the (5) non-AVB queues, queue 4 has highest priority and all protocol control packet (BPDUs)
exchange happens in queue 4. So transmitting data traffic at line rate in queue 4 can cause control packet
(BPDUs) to get dropped and result in loss of protocol convergence. It is recommended to keep the overall
control and data traffic within 25% port speed that are egressing through AVB egress port.

Ensure that the QoS configuration is applied before AVB enablement for proper queueing and bandwidth
allocation. It is suggested not to change the Queue-mapping or add conflicting ACLs when AVB is enabled as
it can lead to undefined traffic-handling, drops, or affect the latency.

If the user-configured Queue-profile is incorrect when AVB is operational, AVB must be disabled, then the
valid 5-Queue profile must be correctly applied, and AVB re-enabled. Do not change the Queue/Schedule
profile when AVB is operational. PCP values for class-A and class-B are fixed to 3 and 2, respectively.

The example queue-profile and schedule-profile configuration is given below:

switch(config)# qos queue-profile newQprofile

switch(config-queue) # map queue 4 local-priority 7

Public

MSRP timer configuration 418

switch(config-queue) # map queue 4 local-priority 6

switch(config-queue) # map queue 3 local-priority 5

switch(config-queue) # map queue 3 local-priority 4

switch(config-queue) # map queue 2 local-priority 3

switch(config-queue) # map queue 2 local-priority 2

switch(config-queue) # map queue 1 local-priority 1

switch(config-queue) # map queue 0 local-priority 0

switch(config-queue)# exit

switch(config-queue)# qos schedule-profile tmp_msrp-profile

switch(config-schedule)# strict queue 4

switch(config-schedule)# dwrr queue 3 weight 1

switch(config-schedule)# dwrr queue 2 weight 1

switch(config-schedule)# dwrr queue 1 weight 1

switch(config-schedule)# dwrr queue 0 weight 1

switch(config-schedule)#exit

switch(config)# apply qos queue-profile newQprofile schedule-profile

tmp_msrp-profile

NOTE

It is suggested to keep the top user-manageable queue (i.e. Queue-4) as Strict-
Priority (SP) to avoid drops in the highest priority non-AVB and control traffic.

AVB caps the maximum reservation to 75% of port bandwidth so that non-AVB traffic is not starved. In case
of congestion or over-subscription, the non-AVB traffic >25% is affected in accordance to the user-defined
queue-mapping with Queue-0 getting the lowest precedence.

Traffic gets remapped on entering an SRP domain carrying the priority value associated with the domain is
forward by the bridge at the domain boundary using a lower priority. Any non-AVB traffic having PCP values
2 and 3 with the same as SRVLAN ID, Cos value is remarked to 0 at the egress.

In case of any replication traffic congestion, packet drops can be seen across the queues/priorities.

Generalized precision time protocol configuration

gPTP (generalized Precision Time Protocol) incorporates the functionality of PTP as defined by IEEE
802.1as, It operates over a LAN network and takes care of timing, synchronization, and PTP functionality
requirements over the AVB network.

AVB requires dot1as profile to be configured for its timing and synchronization requirements. If no PTP
profile is enabled, enabling AVB enables the dot1as profile internally. If any other PTP profile is configured,
AVB enablement fails, the PTP profile must be removed or fixed and AVB must be re-enabled. Removing or
disabling the dot1as profile results in AVB going to non-operational.

VLAN configuration

AVB enable internally enables the AVB VLAN if it is not configured. AVB VLAN is a mandatory requirement
for the functionality. Removal of AVB VLAN results in AVB going to non-operational. If MVRP is enabled
globally, then AVB enable does not internally enable the static VLAN and waits for AVB VLAN to be created
dynamically. If AVB is enabled first followed by MVRP, then the static VLAN is already created by AVB

Public

QoS Configuration 419

enable. In this scenario, AVB continues to operate with the static VLAN. Manually remove the AVB static
VLAN using no vlan if dynamic creation of AVB VLAN (through MVRP registration) is expected.

Feature exclusions

•  AVB supports static or dynamic (based on MVRP registration) AVB VLAN configuration and only one

AVB VLAN is supported.

•  User should not configure the following for AVB:

◦  VSF

◦  VSX (ISL and MCLAGs)

◦  Split cables

◦  MTU, EEE, Flow control

AVB commands 420

avb

Syntax

avb

no avb

Description

Enables the AVB feature and creates the AVB context. Enables user to exercise various configuration options
available under AVB.

The no form of this command removed the AVB configuration in the global context.

Examples

Enable AVB:

switch(config)# avb

switch(config-avb)#
Disable AVB:

switch(config)# no avb

AVB configuration will be deleted.

Continue (y/n)? y

max fan in ports

Syntax

max-fan-in-ports <NUMPORTS>

no max-fan-in-ports <NUMPORTS>

Description

Configures the AVB maximum number of fan in ports globally. The default maximum is 10. Whenever the
max-fan-in-ports is changed, AVB disable and enable are required to make it operational.

Public

max fan in ports 423

The no form of this command sets the AVB maximum number of fan in ports to default.

Parameter

Description

Default: 10.

<NUMPORTS>

Disable and Enable AVB to reflect max-fan-in-ports configuration as

operational.

switch(config-avb)# no max-fan-in-ports 3

operational.

msrp timer join

Syntax

msrp timer join <TIME-INTERVAL>

no msrp timer join <TIME-INTERVAL>

Public

msrp timer join 425

Description

Configures the MSRP join timer on a MSRP enabled interface. The timer configures the time to wait for the
MSRP Protocol Data Units (PDUs) to be sent out of the interface. The timer units are in centiseconds. The
default is 20 centiseconds.

The no form of this command sets the configuration back to the default value.

Parameter

Description

Specifies the time interval in centiseconds. Range: 20‐100. De
fault: 20.

<TIME‐INTERVAL>

Examples

Configure MSRP timer join to 50 centiseconds:

switch(config-if)# msrp timer join 50

Remove MSRP timer join configuration and set to default timers:

switch(config-if)# no msrp timer join 50
